title: Classic Brewing Water Profiles
description: Historical and style-representative water profiles. All values in ppm (mg/L).

profiles.name: Burton-on-Trent
profiles.notes: Extremely high sulfate makes Burton water ideal for hop-forward English ales. The 'Burton snatch' — a sulfury note from high sulfate — is considered part of the character of Burton-style beers.

profiles.name: Dublin
profiles.notes: High bicarbonate balances the roasted malt acidity in dark beers. Low sulfate and chloride keep the profile clean.

profiles.name: Pilsen (Plzeň)
profiles.notes: One of the softest brewing waters in the world. Essential for the Saaz hop softness and pale color of Czech Pilsners. Very difficult to replicate with hard water — RO or distilled water is usually the best starting point.

profiles.name: Munich
profiles.notes: High carbonate traditional Munich water was historically used for dark beers. Modern Munich brewers often treat their water for pale styles.

profiles.name: Vienna
profiles.notes: Moderately high sulfate with balanced minerality.

profiles.name: London
profiles.notes: Moderate alkalinity and high sodium contribute to the full-bodied, malty character of London-style ales.

profiles.name: Edinburgh
profiles.notes: High bicarbonate and calcium. Scottish ales are known for their soft, malty character.

profiles.name: Dortmund
profiles.notes: Hard water with balanced minerals. The Dortmunder Export style has a firm body and moderate bitterness that reflects this profile.
